content:
# Final Review Proposal
**Producer:** agent:claude/claude-opus-4-6

## Summary

The implementation is solid and covers all acceptance criteria. Retry limits are correctly increased (TransportFailure: 5, SchemaValidation: 3, Timeout: 3), BinaryNotFound is terminal, exponential backoff with jitter is implemented and capped at 60s, timeout enforcement via tokio::time::timeout is in place, and the pre-backoff snapshot persistence prevents crash-during-backoff from stranding runs. Resume state correctly preserves retry attempt counters across restarts. All tests pass including the feature-gated workflow engine tests. Three amendments proposed: (1) extract the duplicated `is_timeout_related` pattern into a helper to prevent desync, (2) reconsider the unconfirmed-teardown classification from TransportFailure to Timeout for consistent retry budgets, and (3) add a bounded timeout to `spawn_background_reap` to prevent indefinite task leaks.

## Amendments

1. The `is_timeout_related` pattern-matching block is duplicated verbatim at `src/adapters/process_backend.rs:1273-1284` and `src/adapters/process_backend.rs:1325-1336`. Extract it into a helper method (e.g. `fn is_timeout_related(err: &AppError) -> bool`) to reduce maintenance risk — if the timeout detection heuristic changes (e.g. a new error variant), only one site needs updating instead of two.
   Rationale: This is a moderate maintainability risk. The string-match heuristic (`details.contains("exceeded timeout")`) is fragile: if the error message wording changes in `spawn_and_wait` but only one of the two call sites is updated, timeout artifacts will silently stop being preserved in the stale-session-retry path. A single helper eliminates this class of desync bug.
2. When `confirm_teardown` fails (unconfirmed teardown), the timeout at `src/adapters/process_backend.rs:1077-1083` is classified as `TransportFailure` instead of `Timeout`. This means it gets 5 retries (TransportFailure budget) instead of 3 (Timeout budget), and the retry policy considers it a different failure class than a clean timeout. Consider introducing a dedicated `FailureClass::UnconfirmedTimeout` or keeping the classification as `Timeout` and adding a boolean flag for teardown status — so the retry budget is consistent regardless of whether the kill was confirmed.
   Rationale: An unconfirmed-teardown timeout is semantically still a timeout — the backend process exceeded its time limit. Classifying it as TransportFailure inflates its retry budget from 3 to 5, and more importantly, a run that hits 3 clean timeouts then 1 unconfirmed timeout will get a fresh set of 5 TransportFailure attempts rather than being stopped. The `is_timeout_related` check in the artifact-preservation path already treats both classes the same, which reveals the design intent is that they're the same kind of failure — the classification should match.
3. In `spawn_background_reap` (`src/adapters/process_backend.rs:1557-1563`), the `child.wait().await` has no timeout. If a process is truly unkillable (e.g., uninterruptible I/O state on Linux), the tokio task blocks indefinitely and the `Arc<ManagedChild>` is never dropped. Consider adding a bounded timeout (e.g., 30s) after `force_kill`, and if it expires, log a warning and drop the handle anyway so the task completes.
   Rationale: While truly unkillable processes are rare, in a long-running system these leaked tasks accumulate. Each holds an Arc<ManagedChild> and the invocation_id string, and the tokio task slot is permanently consumed. A bounded fallback ensures the system degrades gracefully rather than accumulating zombie reaper tasks.
